decision: Which architectural style should the system adopt: single-deployment monolith, modular monolith with enforced internal module boundaries, or some other single-server style?

grounding.existing_system: n/a — greenfield (C3): no existing system to conform to
grounding.note: arch-canon.json absent; option set reasoned from established architecture first principles; currency self-verified; multi-service and distributed options excluded as non-compliant with INV6/A13

options.summary: All application code lives in a single deployable unit with no enforced internal module boundaries beyond language-level conventions.
options.reason_to_consider: Simplest possible structure for a personal-tool-scale web app; eliminates all cross-module coordination overhead and is the least build complexity under C2's two-month timeline.

strengths: Minimal project scaffolding and build configuration — fastest to a working skeleton under C2 (two-month timeline).
No inter-module contract overhead; all code in one place simplifies early debugging and feature iteration.
Adequate for A13 scale (tens of users, low concurrency) — no performance benefit from structure at this scale.
Deployment pipeline is straightforward: one artifact, one process, one target (FD4).
costs: No enforced boundary between functional domains (auth, projects, time logging, invoicing); coupling grows organically as features are added.
Refactoring cost rises as the codebase grows — boundaries become harder to draw retroactively.
No mechanical guarantee that, e.g., the invoice module cannot directly call the auth module's internal state; discipline is the only guardrail.
If a modular structure is desired later, the extraction effort is non-trivial.

options.option: Modular monolith with enforced internal module boundaries
options.summary: Single deployable unit whose internal code is divided into explicit modules (e.g. auth, projects, time, invoices) with defined public interfaces and enforced cross-module access rules.
options.reason_to_consider: Preserves single-deployment simplicity (stays compliant with INV6/A13) while providing internal structure that matches the aPRD's distinct functional domains (R2, R3, R5, R6, R7), making the codebase easier to navigate and extend slice by slice.

strengths: Maintains single-server deployment — fully compliant with INV6 and A13; no distributed-systems complexity.
Internal module boundaries map naturally onto the aPRD's functional domains (auth, projects, time logging, invoices), supporting clean slice development.
Enforced public interfaces prevent unintended cross-domain coupling, reducing the risk of entanglement as slices accumulate.
Refactoring to a different service topology later (if scale ever changes) is cheaper with explicit boundaries than with a flat monolith.
One deployable artifact — deployment pipeline is as simple as the flat monolith variant.
costs: Higher initial setup cost than a flat monolith: module boundaries, public-API conventions, and enforcement mechanisms must be established up front.
Enforcement tooling varies by language/framework; some ecosystems make boundary rules easy (e.g. Java packages), others require linting plugins or structural conventions.
Risk of over-engineering boundaries for a personal-tool-scale project (A13) where the added structure may never pay off if the codebase stays small.
More scaffolding decisions to make before the skeleton is buildable, which consumes some of C2's two-month window.

no_pick_note: Live, unranked alternatives. EVALUATE-DECIDE scores against CONSTRAINTS / ACCEPTANCE / NFRs and picks (role 4); OPTION-GEN does not recommend.
